content:
Enrique Dehaerne ,a,b,* Bappaditya Dey ,b Victor Blanco ,b and Jesse Davisa
aKU Leuven, Department of Computer Science, Leuven, Belgium
bIMEC, Angstrom Patterning Department, Leuven, Belgium
ABSTRACT.
We identified, categorized, and discussed automatic defect inspection algorithms
that analyze scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images for semiconductor manu-
facturing (SM). This is a topic of critical importance for the SM industry as the con-
tinuous shrinking of device patterns has led to increasing defectivity and a greater
prevalence of higher-resolution imaging tools such as SEM. Among others, these
aspects threaten to increase costs due to increased inspection time-to-solution and
decreased yield. Relevant research papers were systematically identified in four
popular publication databases in January 2024. A total of 103 papers were selected
after screening for contributions relating to automatic SEM image analysis algo-
rithms for semiconductor defect inspection. We then categorized based on the
inspection tasks they addressed, their evaluation metrics, and the type of algorithms
used. A notable finding from this categorization is that reference-based defect detec-
tion algorithms were the most popular algorithm type until 2020 when deep learning
(DL)-based inspection algorithms became more popular, especially for defect clas-
sification. Furthermore, four broader research questions were discussed to come to
the following conclusions: (i) the key components of inspection algorithms are set
up, pre-processing, feature extraction, and final prediction; (ii) the maturity of the
manufacturing process affects the data availability and required sensitivity of inspec-
tion algorithms; (iii) key challenges for these algorithms relate to the desiderata of
minimizing time-to-solution which pushes for high imaging throughput, reducing
manual input during algorithm setup, and higher processing throughput; and
(iv) three promising directions for future work are suggested based on gaps in the
reviewed literature that address key remaining limitations.
Keywords: optical inspection; metrology; computer vision; electron microscopy;
image processing; semiconductors
Paper 24079V received Oct. 11, 2024; revised Apr. 4, 2025; accepted Apr. 15, 2025; published May
15, 2025.
1 Introduction
Advanced semiconductor manufacturing (SM) lies at the heart of advancing computer chip
technology, aiming to produce more sophisticated and economically viable chips. These chips
comprise many interconnected electrical components, notably transistors, forming complex elec-
tronic circuits. Central to this endeavor is the drive to minimize the size of these components,
historically correlating with significant gains in power, performance, and area.1 Leveraging
wafer-manufacturing techniques, wherein multiple chips (also known as dies on wafer) are fab-
ricated concurrently, further underscores the economic advantage of miniaturization. Patterning,
*Address all correspondence to Enrique Dehaerne, enrique.dehaerne@kuleuven.be
section: Scanning electron microscopy-based automatic > defect inspection for semiconductor > manufacturing: a systematic review
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
the process of creating the structures on the chip that form and connect electrical devices,
is therefore the key challenge for advanced SM.
Defects are any errors in patterning or other processes that lower the chip manufacturing
yield. High yield must be achieved to make SM economically viable. There are many different
potential causes for defects including particle contamination, improper process tool parameter
settings, or photon-chemical stochastics (becoming increasingly prominent as pattern dimen-
sions shrink2). Generally, defects are more common in the early stages of process development.
Finding and analyzing defects, or defect inspection, is therefore critical to determine the cause of
the defects so that the manufacturing process can be refined appropriately to achieve higher
yields in subsequent wafers (see Fig. 1).
Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) is very popular for defectivity inspection due to
its relatively high resolution, throughput, and versatility. As structures on chips continue to
shrink, SEM is quickly becoming more important relative to optical microscopy (OM) thanks
to its superior resolution. Although SEM is slower than OM, it achieves a better trade-off
between throughput and resolution compared with other high-resolution imaging methods such
as atomic force microscopy (AFM).3 Both SEM and OM are versatile in that they can be used to
measure anywhere on wafers and photomasks. Different imaging modes, most notably voltage
contrast (VC)4,5 and multiple perspective SEM images (MPSI),6 make SEM particularly useful in
certain scenarios such as buried defects and contrast between different materials, respectively.
The combination of all these characteristics is what makes SEM a critical tool for many inspec-
tion jobs, especially at advanced technology nodes.
Algorithms that process and analyze SEM images enable automation of inspection tasks.
This is desirable because manual image analysis is very time-consuming for experts and has even
been found to have poor repeatability.7,8 Automatic defect inspection promises to relieve experts
of the image analysis task, allowing them to focus on optimizing the process using insights from
the automated inspection. Research interest in novel inspection algorithms for SEM-based
imaging has increased in recent years. This is thanks to various factors such as mounting talent
pressures,9 smaller pattern dimensions that add challenges for conventional algorithms, and
the overall increased importance of research in SM. Automated defect inspection ultimately
improves yield while reducing turnaround time.
This systematic review makes significant contributions to the field by categorizing selected
papers and addressing key research questions based on our analysis of them. To achieve this,
we first identified relevant papers through a comprehensive search of the four most popular
publication databases (IEEE, SPIE, ScienceDirect, AIP) using specific inclusion and exclusion
criteria. Through this process, we selected 103 papers for review, which is significantly more
than related systematic reviews.10,11 These reviews exclude algorithm types that we include, such
as no learning (NL) reference-based algorithms (see Sec. 4.1). We then categorized the papers
based on the inspection tasks they address, the types of algorithms they employ, and the metrics
used for algorithm evaluation (Secs. 3 to 5). In addition, we provide a detailed discussion and
comparison of the strengths and weaknesses of these categories in their respective sections.
Finally, we synthesize new findings from the selected papers to answer the following research
questions (Sec. 6):
Fig. 1 Flowchart showing a simplified overview of the patterning process which relies on defect
inspection to guide process optimization.

J. Micro/Nanopattern. Mater. Metrol.

Apr–Jun 2025 • Vol. 24(2)

RQ1 What are the key components for SEM-based semiconductor defect inspection
algorithms?
RQ2 How does the context of the manufacturing process affect defect inspection algorithms?
RQ3 What are the key challenges for these algorithms?
RQ4 What are promising future research directions for the field?
2 Methodology
This review used a systematic methodology, following Preferred Reporting Elements for
Systematic Reviews and Meta-analyses (PRISMA) guidelines where appropriate,12 for selecting
relevant papers. The methodology can be divided into three main steps, each explained further in
the following subsections.
section: Scanning electron microscopy-based automatic > J. Micro/Nanopattern. Mater. Metrol.
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
2.1 Relevancy Criteria and Search Terms

First, a list of criteria was created to determine which algorithms discussed in the papers would be
included in the review. These criteria were as follows:
section: Scanning electron microscopy-based automatic > 2.1 Relevancy Criteria and Search Terms

content:
1. Automatic image analysis: the algorithm must be able to analyze images without requir-

ing input from human operators. Human control in the form of adjustment of parameters or
labeling samples does not violate this criterion as long as it does not have to be done for
every image at prediction time.
section: 1. Automatic image analysis: the algorithm must be able to analyze images without requir-
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
2. Analysis of SEM images: the algorithm must be intended to be used on SEM images.

We also accept variations [for example, transmission electron microscopy (TEM)] to the
traditional SEM imaging mode and mention these variations in the discussion of our
review when relevant. Papers that experimented with images acquired from other tools
such as optical, spectroscopy, acoustic, or physical probing tools were not considered
unless they were also explicitly used on SEM images in the paper. Papers that only used
simulated data were also excluded. Other types of data (e.g., design layouts) can be used
as long as SEM images are also used in the paper.
section: 2. Analysis of SEM images: the algorithm must be intended to be used on SEM images.
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
3. Semiconductor defect inspection: the algorithm must directly produce outputs that deter-

mine the defectivity on wafers and/or photomasks. Algorithms for analyzing defects for
failure analysis were also considered. Algorithms for other manufacturing quality inspec-
tion tasks, such as measurements of critical dimension (CD), pattern fidelity, or overlay
error, are not considered unless they explicitly enable defectivity analysis in addition to
these other tasks.

to maximize purity, i.e., the number of relevant records retrieved over the total number of records
retrieved. Furthermore, an attempt was made to mitigate potential biases toward certain subsets
of relevant articles. However, certain search terms had to be changed to optimize the purity of

the retrieval. For example, the “photomask” search term replaced “mask” because “mask”
retrieved many more non-relevant publications. This could have led to fewer mask inspection
papers relative to wafer inspection papers that are representative of the field. Still, the search
command reflects our best attempt to retrieve as many relevant articles as possible from the
available literature.
section: 4. Novel contribution: literature reviews and surveys were not considered. > J. Micro/Nanopattern. Mater. Metrol.

content:
Second, popular publication databases were identified by counting the number of papers
retrieved from the Google Scholar search engine using the search term from Table 1. Google
Scholar was not used to directly search for relevant papers because it retrieved too many results
to screen exhaustively (600 k+) and has limited search functionality to filter these results.
The publishers of the first 150 papers retrieved were counted. The most contributing publishers
were SPIE (47), IEEE (37), Elsevier (12), AIP (11), and IOP (9). These were selected for paper
identification on their respective search engines except for IOP because of their limited search
functionality. All other publishers had four or fewer papers retrieved. This search was performed
on October 30, 2023.
Although this method intuitively identifies publishers that publish many potentially relevant
records, it does not consider purity. It is possible that certain publishers do not publish many
relevant publications for this review. Consequently, they are not elected as a paper database to be
thoroughly searched yet they may have a high purity. Ultimately, the total number of retrieved
articles was chosen as the only publisher selection criterion because considering purity at this
stage would require additional screening of papers and would risk excluding large publishers that
publish many similar but non-relevant papers.
section: 4. Novel contribution: literature reviews and surveys were not considered. > 2.2 Identification of Popular Publication Databases
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

Potentially relevant papers were retrieved from the selected publishers’ respective search engines
and screened to ensure they met the relevancy criteria for this review. Figure 2 shows a flowchart
of this process and the number of records/papers at each step. Note that a record in this case refers
to the title and abstract of a full paper, which is either a conference proceedings paper or a journal
article. Minor modifications to the search command in Table 1, and additional filters were applied
for each search engine where appropriate (see This review in the Supplementary material13).
All searches were performed on January 4, 2024. Only records with a publication year of 2023
or earlier were retrieved.
The steps followed to remove irrelevant papers from results retrieved from each search
engine were as follows:

content:
3 Defect Inspection Tasks
Because the terminology and definitions for defect inspection tasks vary between the selected
papers, we define these tasks and categorize them. Figure 3 shows the number of papers that use
algorithms for three main types of tasks and their sub-types. At a high level, defect inspection
algorithms are tasked with defect detection (whether there are defects in an image) and classi-
fication (what type of defects are in an image). A third, lesser-known task is severity estimation,
which estimates how likely it is that a defect will affect yield. These outputs help operators decide
how to best mitigate defects in subsequent wafers or photomasks or continue processing the
current wafer. Figures 4 and 5 show example predictions for each main task type and their
sub-types. These task (sub-)types are further defined and discussed in the rest of this section.
section: 3. Full papers screening: papers for all 564 records that passed initial screening were > J. Micro/Nanopattern. Mater. Metrol.
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
3.1 Defect Detection

Defects are detected in images at either the image or instance level. Image-level detection algo-
rithms take a full SEM image as input and output a binary signal indicating whether the image
contains a defect. This limited output information is generally only useful when assumptions
about the input image have been made. This usually includes the assumptions that there is only
Fig. 2 Flowchart of the paper identification and screening steps followed. The numbers of records/
papers included and excluded at each step are also shown.
Dehaerne et al.: Scanning electron microscopy-based automatic defect inspection. . .
section: 3. Full papers screening: papers for all 564 records that passed initial screening were > 3.1 Defect Detection
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
one defect, often of a particular class,14–16 or that the exact number of defects is not important.
More generally, images can contain many defect instances and the number and characteristics of
each instance may be important. This information can be obtained from localization algorithms
that indicate where defects are located in images. The popular types of localization algorithms
predict either defect segmentations or bounding boxes.
Fig. 3 Number of papers that address task (sub-)types.
Fig. 4 Patterned wafer SEM image with example predictions for the image-level detection, image-
level classification, and severity estimation tasks. The bold black text indicates the correct predic-
tion for the given image, whereas the other possible predictions are in gray.
Fig. 5 Example SEM image input (left) with example defect segmentation and bounding box
predictions (center). Each of these defect predictions can be classified resulting in an instance-
level classification (right).
Dehaerne et al.: Scanning electron microscopy-based automatic defect inspection. . .
section: 3. Full papers screening: papers for all 564 records that passed initial screening were > J. Micro/Nanopattern. Mater. Metrol.
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
The most popular approach to localizing defects refers to predicting the location of a defect on
the pixel level. In principle, segmentations provide the highest resolution location of defects.
Therefore, segmentations are most useful when accurate 2D dimensions for defects are required.
However, correctly predicting exactly which pixels in an image belong to defect instances is
challenging. For example, determining the precise edges of patterns can be difficult17–20 and
ambiguous.21 In addition, many small pattern variations can resemble actual defects, necessitating
the filtering of false positive (FP), or nuisance, predictions on the pixel or instance levels (see
Sec. 4.1.1). Despite these challenges, segmenting defects and related tasks such as edge detection
are crucial parts of many inspection algorithms, as they provide maximal localization information.
A special type of localization is identifying the location of defects on an image linescan.
However, this is not a popular or particularly interesting type of detection due to its heavy reli-
ance on assumptions about pattern structure in the row of pixels being scanned. It will not
be discussed further other than noting that the two selected papers that do linescan-based
detection22,23 are categorized as segmentation because it is more similar to this task than others.
section: 3. Full papers screening: papers for all 564 records that passed initial screening were > J. Micro/Nanopattern. Mater. Metrol. > 3.1.1 Segmentation
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
Another way for algorithms to localize defects is by identifying bounding boxes, which are
rectangular (or circular in one selected paper24) patches of an image that contain a defect.
Bounding boxes are intended to indicate a patch in the input image where the defect lies but
not precisely, which pixels in that patch describe the defect.
Many works start from a segmentation prediction and then predict bounding boxes from
it.25–31 We assume that this extra step was implemented because it mitigates the more difficult
task of predicting defectivity for every pixel. Other possible motivations for this are to connect
separated segmentation components that belong to the same defect, which might otherwise be
counted as entirely different defect instances, or to simplify downstream processing and analysis.
Other works analyze images in a patch-wise manner where the image patch is then consid-
ered to be a bounding box if a defect is found in it.32–34 This simplifies a complex localization
problem into a set of binary image-level detection problems. We should note that these selected
papers use fixed patch sizes which are chosen based on assumptions of the size of the defects
in the images. This makes these methods difficult to generalize to different scenarios such as
varying microscope magnification.
Direct prediction of bounding boxes of variable size would therefore be preferred and is only
achieved in the selected papers by certain supervised deep learning (DL) algorithms known more
broadly as object-detection algorithms that learn how to predict bounding-box dimensions from
labels provided by human experts (see Sec. 4.3). An interesting subset of these algorithms are
instance-segmentation algorithms, which first predict bounding boxes and then predict the
segmentation of the defects within each bounding box, striking an optimal balance between
high-resolution localization and being able to distinguish between individual defect instances.
In conclusion, bounding box prediction is generally most useful for accurately recognizing
individual defect instances, which can be tricky to deduce from pixel-level segmentation
predictions. However, bounding box localization is not suitable for use cases where pixel-level
localization precision is needed unless it is combined with segmentation.
section: 3. Full papers screening: papers for all 564 records that passed initial screening were > J. Micro/Nanopattern. Mater. Metrol. > 3.1.2 Bounding box
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
Knowing more about a defect than simply whether it is a defect or not is often crucial for deter-
mining how to modify a process to mitigate it. Most defect classification algorithms predict
which class a given input belongs to from a predefined set of distinct classes.
The input can be a full SEM image or an individual defect instance. For full-image inputs,
it is often already assumed that the image has a defect in it. For example, fast throughput tools
such as OM can flag potential defects but cannot classify them due to their low imaging
resolution. These potential detected defects are then imaged at higher resolution using SEM
to facilitate classification.35,36 Instance-level classification is most often performed on individual
defect instances obtained from the output of a localization algorithm. Other instance-level
Dehaerne et al.: Scanning electron microscopy-based automatic defect inspection. . .
section: 3. Full papers screening: papers for all 564 records that passed initial screening were > 3.2 Defect Classification
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
classification algorithms perform localization and classification simultaneously, offering advan-
tages such as implementation simplicity and increased processing throughput. A potential dis-
advantage of this approach is that it restricts the types of defects that can be localized to those
belonging to the set of classes the algorithm is programmed to classify.31
In many classification tasks, the set of possible defect classes has an underlying structure that
can be exploited by the classification algorithm. A set of classifiers mimicking a decision tree
structure can improve performance when the set of possible defect classes is hierarchical.37–40
An example of this is using an initial classifier to distinguish between broad defect classes such
as particle contaminants or pattern structure defects and then selecting a fine-grained classifier,
specialized in distinguishing between particle contaminants for example, based on the prediction
of the initial.37 Another possible advantage of this method includes the reusability of coarse-
grained classifiers for different classification problems. The main disadvantage of this method
is that the setup of many dependent classifiers can be more difficult than creating a single
classifier.
section: 3. Full papers screening: papers for all 564 records that passed initial screening were > J. Micro/Nanopattern. Mater. Metrol.
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
Some papers estimate the severity of a defect by assigning it a value that represents either the
probability of the defect affecting yield41 or some related measure such as the resistance added by
a defect.42 This is similar to classification but differs in that the output is a continuous value or an
element in an ordered set, which does not directly provide information that could distinguish
other characteristics such as the cause of a defect. Aspects that can affect the severity of a defect
include contact with patterns43 and deviations in expected pattern dimensions41,44–49 or pixel-
value intensities related to material properties.42,50,51 Two papers discuss algorithms that predict
3D measurements for defects, which we categorize as severity classification algorithms because
this type of information is more linked to severity than other task types. It seems to us that, on
their own, continuous defect severity estimate values are primarily useful as detailed analysis data
for process engineers. Actionable predictions for fully automated inspection flows will most
likely require thresholding these estimated values into an ordered set of ordinal predictions that
indicate the severity of the problem and give guidance on how to proceed.
4 Types of Inspection Algorithms
Different types of algorithms can be used for each defect inspection task. We categorize these
algorithm types primarily by what data they use. The three main types we defined in this sense
are NL, machine learning (ML), and DL. Figure 6 shows the number of papers associated with
these types and their sub-types. In general, each algorithm (sub-)type can be used for any pre-
viously defined defect inspection tasks but some are better suited for certain tasks. This section
discusses these and other factors affecting the performance of inspection algorithms.
section: 3. Full papers screening: papers for all 564 records that passed initial screening were > 3.3 Defect Severity Estimation
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
4.1 No Learning

NL refers to algorithms that are not trained on any data and are instead developed manually.
This means that all the parameters of NL algorithms must be initialized using expert knowledge
and refined manually through trial and error.52 This process does not require a representative
training dataset, which can be a great advantage.37 However, manual refinement can be difficult
and time-consuming.53,54 In any case, the selected papers seem to suggest that NL algorithms
were the most popular algorithm category until about 2020 (see Fig. 7).
section: 3. Full papers screening: papers for all 564 records that passed initial screening were > 4.1 No Learning
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
4.1.1 Reference-based

References provide information about the expected patterns that can be compared with a query
image to detect defects. We define reference data as data that does not come directly from the
query image (i.e., the sample to be inspected). In addition, reference data refer to data used
directly at prediction time, unlike data used to train ML models at setup time.
References can be binary image representations of pattern design information. Segmenting
the pattern or extracting pattern edges from a query image and then comparing it with the design
data is the most common method of using reference data. The left side of Fig. 8 shows a
Dehaerne et al.: Scanning electron microscopy-based automatic defect inspection. . .
section: 3. Full papers screening: papers for all 564 records that passed initial screening were > 4.1 No Learning > 4.1.1 Reference-based
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
simplified example of this method. Another approach is to synthesize SEM images from design
data using simulators55,56 or deep neural networks (DNNs)52 and compare these synthesized
images with the query image. A simplified example of this is shown on the right side of Fig. 8.
Both methods leverage the availability of an objective reference of an ideal pattern, and the
provided design data can be aligned with the pattern in the query image.
Comparison with the design primarily enables defect localization, but the design reference
data can also be used for multiclass classification. Eight papers20,26,27,36,52,57–59 use design infor-
mation to infer defect classes based on the relative location of defects and target design structures.
For example, extra structures could be nuisance defects if they do not interact with the intended
structures’ electrical characteristics of the target features and this can be determined through
the use of design data.52,57 Four of these papers26,27,58,59 specifically mention using multi-layer
design data to classify defects in such a way that the root cause can be better traced to a particular
process layer.
Fig. 6 Sunburst chart showing the number of papers that used different algorithm (sub-)types.
Fig. 7 Number of papers per year categorized by algorithm type(s) used.
Dehaerne et al.: Scanning electron microscopy-based automatic defect inspection. . .
section: 3. Full papers screening: papers for all 564 records that passed initial screening were > J. Micro/Nanopattern. Mater. Metrol.
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
However, design reference-based methods also have limitations. One main limitation is
overcoming the domain gap between the ideal design pattern and a real pattern printed on
a wafer. Many wafer pattern features, such as corner rounding and edge roughness, are not actual
defects but can be mistaken for them because they differ from the target design reference. Some
works compensate for this by smoothing design polygons before reference comparison34,57,59
and/or determining appropriate margins of error for edge differences between the reference and
query images.20,26,27,32,45,48,60–63 SEM image synthesis from design data can mitigate this but only
if the patterning process of the query image is simulated accurately enough. Although not
mentioned explicitly by these works, another disadvantage of design reference-based methods
according to us is the high memory and computational costs associated with processing large
volumes of design data. From this, we can infer that these algorithms typically need to be run
on remote high-performance computing (HPC) servers. This can extend the time to solution
(see also Sec. 6.3) and poses data security risks due to the sharing of valuable semiconductor
product designs with external HPC service providers.
SEM images can also be used directly as references to mitigate the domain-gap problem
from using design reference data. The basic process of SEM reference defect segmentation
is similar to the process of design reference comparison after SEM synthesis (see the right side
of Fig. 8). SEM images taken from qualified products are excellent references because they
include non-defective deviations from the target design, making them more similar to the query
image while not including deviations that are true defects.62,64 When no references from qualified
products are available, SEM references can be obtained from the same die location on other dies
on the wafer (commonly referred to as die-to-die inspection).65 Alternatively, other locations on
the same die that have the same target pattern can be used.66,67 It is usually assumed that refer-
ences are defect-free, implying that an initial reference selection stage is needed before being able
to run the SEM reference algorithm. Two works65,66 relax this assumption by averaging many
reference images to obtain a reference that is likely to be defect-free even if few of the individual
references have defects. Lee and Yoo68 used multiple references to improve defect segmentation
accuracy. Three papers25,69,70 use MPSI, which captures multiple images from different detectors
simultaneously, to improve localization performance by aggregating comparisons from multiple
perspectives of the reference and query. In our view, an inherent limitation of SEM references
from non-qualified references is that systematic defects that occur frequently over many dies
are not detected, as references taken from these dies will also contain these defects. Still,
SEM references can be a valuable alternative to design references especially when design data
cannot be used.
The main disadvantage of both design and SEM reference-based algorithms is that they
operate on extremely local features, mostly at the pixel level. Therefore, these algorithms are
generally sensitive to small differences between the query and the reference. This includes the
differences described above such as pattern roughness but also includes alignment between the
query and reference image. Although proper alignment is usually assumed as a given by many
Fig. 8 Simplified overview of design reference-based defect inspection. Given an aligned pair of
a binary design reference image and a query SEM image as inputs, a defect segmentation can be
obtained by either (left) a difference image of the design and a pattern segmentation from the query
or (right) synthesizing a gray-scale SEM image from the design, taking the absolute difference of
the image, and thresholding to get a binary difference image. Postprocessing is usually performed
to remove nuisance predictions, morphological transformations were used for postprocessing in
these examples.
Dehaerne et al.: Scanning electron microscopy-based automatic defect inspection. . .

content:
papers, it is a challenging problem addressed by three of the selected papers.71–73 Two papers
mitigate the need for perfect alignment by operating on a kernel space rather than directly on the
pixel space.70,74 These papers mention that many kernel functions are possible. Two papers used
DL-based methods to use reference data on a learned feature space without direct pixel comparison.
We believe this is a promising direction for future reference-based methods (see Sec. 6.4.3).
In summary, research in NL reference-based algorithms focused mostly on pre- and postprocess-
ing of difference images to compensate for the limitations of only considering local features,
whereas few works attempted to mitigate this by working on higher-level feature spaces instead.
section: 3. Full papers screening: papers for all 564 records that passed initial screening were > J. Micro/Nanopattern. Mater. Metrol.
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
NL algorithms that do not use references are more specialized for certain use cases. This also
means that the types of tasks they are used for are very diverse. For example, both 3D defect
measurement papers use NL algorithms without references. Wang et al.75 detect crystal defects in
atomic-resolution TEM images of semiconductor materials using an NL algorithm that extracts
phases from the periodic structures in the crystals. The separate perspectives captured by MPSI
are useful for no reference NL algorithms to extract particular features from each perspective for
defect severity estimation37,43 or classification.76 Another relatively popular scenario for NL algo-
rithms that do not use references is comparing gray-scale pixel values to expected values. This is
most common with VC imaging22,23,51,77 where gray-scale values correlate directly to differences
in potential which can be indicative of certain types of defects such as buried defects. However,
expected gray-scale values can change significantly between processes and patterns, forcing
these methods to calibrate to the target dataset. In this case, ML or DL algorithms would not
be a good alternative if there is no representative training data available a priori. This again
highlights the specialized nature of no reference methods, which only rely on samples of target
data and expert knowledge.
section: 3. Full papers screening: papers for all 564 records that passed initial screening were > J. Micro/Nanopattern. Mater. Metrol. > 4.1.2 No reference
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
ML algorithms are trained on a dataset to make predictions on unseen data. This (partially) mit-
igates the highly specialized nature of NL methods by learning expected feature values from the
dataset instead of deriving values from expertise and trial-and-error. This confers the advantage
that ML models can process high-dimensional quantitative data more efficiently than humans,
allowing them to find more complex patterns in this data more quickly. Once a dataset has been
obtained the ML model training is also automatic, meaning that operators are free to spend time
on other tasks while it is training.
However, creating a dataset can require significant effort. Feature extraction is mostly still
performed using NL methods meaning that many limitations of NL methods carry over to the ML
methods that use them. Some features can be extracted relatively easily from NL methods, such
as feature area and perimeter from segmentations (see left side of Fig. 9) or brightness in VC
imaging. However, other, possibly more informative, features can be difficult to extract using NL
methods, limiting ML model performance. Assuming all possible informative features can be
extracted, the selection of the most informative features is also non-trivial. Feature selection
is often performed manually using domain knowledge but can also be done automatically using
Fig. 9 Left: a simple example of a two-dimensional feature space (area and perimeter of the pat-
tern) extracted from images. Right: how a decision tree could partition the space to classify certain
defect types.
Dehaerne et al.: Scanning electron microscopy-based automatic defect inspection. . .
section: 3. Full papers screening: papers for all 564 records that passed initial screening were > 4.2 Machine Learning
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
so-called feature selection algorithms. Gómez-Sirvent et al.78 showed that finding an optimal
subset of features for an ML classifier using feature selection algorithms can be computationally
expensive. Care should also be taken to ensure the dataset has enough samples to avoid over-
fitting, which is when the model fits the dataset too closely and it does not generalize well to new
samples. Still, ML algorithms have proven useful for making predictions for defect inspection
tasks given a representative dataset with appropriate features.
Many types of ML algorithms exist, the ML algorithms used by the selected papers can be
divided into supervised and unsupervised models. All of them were used for classification or
severity estimation tasks. In the rest of this section, we will briefly introduce the important
ML algorithms used in the selected papers.
section: 3. Full papers screening: papers for all 564 records that passed initial screening were > J. Micro/Nanopattern. Mater. Metrol.
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
Supervised ML models are trained using pairs of data samples and labels. Supervised ML models
used in the selected papers include decision trees (DTs),37,43,79–81 support vector machines
(SVMs),34,78,80 k-nearest neighbors (kNNs),79,82,83 and statistical regression (assumed).42 DTs
learn to create rules on the feature space that partition the samples in the training dataset accord-
ing to their labels, as shown in Fig. 9. SVMs also seek to partition the training dataset but achieve
this by defining one or more hyper-planes in a transformed kernel space defined by a kernel
function. These hyper-planes in the kernel space effectively define nonlinear decision boundaries
in the original feature space, as shown in Fig. 10. These partitions can directly correspond to
classes for classification tasks or to continuous output values for severity estimation. kNN algo-
rithms infer labels for an unseen, unlabeled sample based on the distance of the unlabeled sample
to the k-nearest labeled samples. This means that training a kNN algorithm simply consists of
storing all training sample features and inference consists mostly of calculating distances
between the unlabeled sample and all the training samples. Although mostly used for classifi-
cation, distances between samples or partition boundaries can also be used for severity
estimation.43 With enough training data samples with relevant features, all these ML algorithms
should learn a mapping between data samples and corresponding labels for unseen samples.
section: 3. Full papers screening: papers for all 564 records that passed initial screening were > J. Micro/Nanopattern. Mater. Metrol. > 4.2.1 Supervised ML algorithms
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
Unsupervised ML aims to make predictions on data samples without labels, which is advanta-
geous since labels can be difficult or time-consuming to obtain. All three unsupervised ML
papers used the k-means clustering algorithm.35,49,84 Clustering involves defining a distance
metric between features extracted from data samples to indicate how (dis-)similar samples are.
These distances are used to define groups or clusters of samples with similar characteristics.
These papers use clustering for classification49,84 or severity estimation35,49 tasks based on
belonging or distance to a cluster, respectively. A disadvantage of these clusters being fully unsu-
pervised is that the clusters they define can be irrelevant to the particular use case they are being
used for. With proper feature selection and distance metrics, clustering can be useful when no
labels are available.
Fig. 10 Simple example of binary separation of one group of samples using a hyper-plane defined
in the kernel space by an SVM.
Dehaerne et al.: Scanning electron microscopy-based automatic defect inspection. . .
section: 3. Full papers screening: papers for all 564 records that passed initial screening were > J. Micro/Nanopattern. Mater. Metrol. > 4.2.2 Unsupervised ML algorithms
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
DL is a subset of ML that uses multi-layer neural networks, known as DNNs. For most DNNs,
the input is simply an SEM image rather than features extracted from the image similar to other
ML models. This is possible due to the automatic hierarchical feature extraction capability of
DNNs that emerges through the model’s inner layers before a final layer makes a task-specific
prediction. With sufficient training data, DNNs can make accurate predictions without manual
feature engineering.
Figure 6 shows that the most popular type of DNNs is convolutional neural networks
(CNNs). CNNs are specifically designed to exploit characteristics of images, such as relations
between neighboring pixels or translation invariance, which makes them more efficient at learn-
ing to extract useful image features. More concretely, CNNs consist primarily of convolutional
layers that learn convolutional filters that slide across images to extract feature maps. Feature
maps are often downsampled further by a pooling layer. Figure 11 shows feature maps extracted
from an SEM image using a prototypical CNN model. The early layers extract low-level features
such as edges, whereas later layers extract increasingly more abstract features.
The efficient feature extraction capabilities of advanced CNNs have made them so popular
for defect inspection that in 2020 they surpassed NL as the most widely used algorithm category
by the selected papers (see Fig. 7). We suggest that this occurred because CNNs reached a tipping
point where the effort needed to curate and label data for training a viable model in a controlled
experiment became less than the effort required to handcraft NL extraction and prediction algo-
rithms to achieve equivalent accuracy. This data-driven approach could also allow the user to
refine their algorithms more easily over time by adding new data samples to the training dataset,
for example, rather than manually adjusting algorithm parameters or components. However,
in our experience, this popularity has yet to translate to real SM environments. We believe that
a reason for this is a lack of trust due to the high-dimensional features extracted by DL models,
which are not easily interpretable by humans.37 This is particularly true when the target images
are not similar to images in the dataset used to measure the performance of the model. NL algo-
rithms, on the other hand, are inherently interpretable since they were entirely developed by
humans. Other, more well-studied disadvantages of DL relate to practical difficulties in creating
a good training dataset. These difficulties will be discussed more in the upcoming subsections.
In any case, CNNs have garnered significant interest in DL within the SM defect inspection
community.
Transformers, a relatively new DNN architecture,86 have also been used by three selected
studies from 2023.8,87,88 Compared with CNNs, transformers are more computationally efficient
in training and capturing global information but scale poorly with image size and generally
Fig. 11 SEM image with feature maps output from select convolutional layers from a pretrained
VGG16 model.85 The learned filters of the pretrained model for the first layer of the model are also
shown. Each feature map is the result of applying learned convolutional filters over feature maps
from previous layers (or the input image in the case of the first layer).
Dehaerne et al.: Scanning electron microscopy-based automatic defect inspection. . .

source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
J. Micro/Nanopattern. Mater. Metrol.

require more data to achieve the same performance.89,90 Evidently, implementing and adapting
advanced DL-based methods is a popular research direction for SEM-based defect inspection.

content:
4.3.1 Supervised DL algorithms

Supervised DL models are trained using pairs of data samples and labels, similar to supervised
section: 3. Full papers screening: papers for all 564 records that passed initial screening were > J. Micro/Nanopattern. Mater. Metrol. > 4.3.1 Supervised DL algorithms
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
rate classification usually requires highly abstract features that are difficult to extract manually
for ML algorithms but are learned automatically by DNNs. To facilitate this learning, experts
label examples for each class. The main disadvantage of DL is that it generally requires larger
training datasets than ML models. This is limiting in low-data regimes or where labeling costs
are too high. The following paragraphs discuss techniques used by selected papers to reduce
the number of (labeled) training examples required. We finish this discussion by also discussing
label quality.
Data augmentation, modifying data samples to create new ones, is a popular technique used
by 19 of the selected papers.29,30,39,40,54,82,88,91–102 For supervised learning, data augmentation can
reduce the need to label as many data samples. Although most of these works use NL methods for
augmentation, such as random image flipping, two papers53,95 used generative adversarial
CNNs103 to generate new images similar to the training dataset, increasing the number of data
samples and improving the performance of their defect classification CNNs. One selected paper30
manually augmented defect-free images by “painting” defects onto images. In this case, aug-
mentation was used to address the lack of available data rather than saving human labeling time.
Another popular technique to reduce training data requirements is transfer learning. This
refers to taking a DL model trained on data from another distribution and training it further
on data from the target distribution. Along with generally requiring less data and computation
to converge, transfer learning has also been shown to improve generalizability in semiconductor
defect detection.104 Five papers specifically mention using transfer learning from natural image
datasets,39,40,52,88,105 three transfer learning from SEM datasets,7,30,87 and another three transfer
learning from both natural and SEM datasets.8,104,106
Semi-supervised or weakly supervised learning methods use limited labeling time effi-
ciently. Semi-supervised learning involves labeling a limited subset of the training dataset while
the rest is unlabeled. This is useful when there is more data available than can be labeled. Kim
et al.102 learn from both labeled and unlabeled data simultaneously while Lu et al.87 first pretrains
on unlabeled data in an unsupervised manner and then finetunes on the available labeled data.
Both reported improved performance when utilizing unlabeled data compared with only using
the limited labeled data. Weak supervision refers to using labels that do not correspond directly
with the desired output of the model but are easier/faster to obtain. Patel et al.93 use class acti-
vation maps107 that localize defects in an image while only being trained on image-level defect
class labels. Image-level labels are easier to obtain than instance classification labels, allowing
a larger dataset to be labeled within the same time budget. Semi-supervised and weakly super-
vised learning work in different ways to maximize model performance with limited labeling time.
Besides data quantity, labels should also be consistent and as close as possible to the ground
truth to ensure DL model learning converges as intended. Imoto et al.7 found that manual label-
ing of SEM images can be noisy and that this data can be used to train models in a pre-training
stage, but highly reliable labels should be used for the final, fine-tuning stage of training.
Dehaerne et al.100 found that inconsistencies in labelers can have detrimental effects on the train-
ing of a CNN-based defect localization and classification model. They propose a semi-automated
method for aggregating labels from multiple labelers to create a more consistent training dataset.
Both papers show that label quality should not be overlooked when building a training dataset.
section: ML. Supervised DL is most popular for classification which intuitively makes sense since accu-
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
4.3.2 Unsupervised DL

Unsupervised DL can be split into two main groups, reconstruction-based learning and heuristics-
based learning.
Learning to reconstruct input images with incomplete information allows models to
reconstruct certain versions of the input image or extract image features without labels.
Dehaerne et al.: Scanning electron microscopy-based automatic defect inspection. . .
section: ML. Supervised DL is most popular for classification which intuitively makes sense since accu- > 4.3.2 Unsupervised DL
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
Two papers31,108 used DNNs with a bottleneck structure, forcing the model to learn an efficient
feature encoding at the bottleneck layer. When trained exclusively on defect-free images, these
models fail to reconstruct defects during inference. This allows the model to generate defect-
free versions of the input aligned with the input image, which can be used as a reference for
downstream reference-based defect inspection methods. Dey et al.54 train a denoising model by
corrupting pixels in an image at the input. This incomplete information makes the model
unable to reconstruct pixel-wise independent noise from the input, which was found to improve
downstream defect inspection performance. Lu et al.87 trained a feature extractor DNN to
reconstruct parts of the image that are masked at the input given the rest of the image as context.
They use this to pre-train the feature extractor for transfer learning to a downstream supervised
learning phase. These papers suggest that reconstruction-based learning can be used for various
types of processing useful for defect inspection algorithms.
Other unsupervised DL methods use heuristics-based objectives to train models to produce
outputs resembling desired labels. Two papers109,110 use objective functions based on heuristics
such as pattern disorder, contrast, and pixel brightness to train their models. Ofir et al.96 trained
a model using copy-paste augmentation on defect-free images to create synthetic defects.
Although they do not look exactly like real defects, these synthetic defects had enough similar
characteristics for the model to learn to localize real defects at inference time.
Unsupervised learning offers advantages over supervised learning: (i) it eliminates the need
for costly human labeling, and (ii) it avoids biases and false assumptions that can come with
labeled data (as discussed in Sec. 4.3.1). However, with a representative labeled dataset, super-
vised models are expected to perform better than unsupervised models. Ofir et al.96 validated this
in their experiments, and we find this to be generally true based on our knowledge of the broader
DL literature.
5 Evaluation Metrics
Given a task and target dataset, quantitative metrics are crucial for evaluating the performance of
an algorithm and comparing it to other algorithms. As shown in Fig. 12, we categorize quanti-
tative metrics primarily by whether they compare predictions with ground truth labels.
section: ML. Supervised DL is most popular for classification which intuitively makes sense since accu- > J. Micro/Nanopattern. Mater. Metrol.
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
Predictions can be evaluated by categorizing them by the nature of the prediction (true versus
false) and its corresponding ground truth (positive versus negative). This ground truth is usually
obtained from careful manual inspection, similar to how most labeling training datasets for ML
and DL models are labeled. As discussed in Sec. 4.3.1, Dehaerne et al.100 found that the ground
truth labels assigned through manual inspection can differ significantly between experts, sug-
gesting that even manual inspection should be verified. In addition, Neumann et al.31 found that
their method detected more defects than were initially identified manually, so they manually
verified these extra defect detections and recalculated the recall based on these extra verifi-
cations as an additional metric. An alternative to manually reviewing SEM images to obtain
ground truths is using additional, more objective tools such as AFM and electrical testing.
Common limitations of these tools are low throughput, destructivity, and tool availability.
In general, care should be taken to ensure reliable ground truth data is available for comparison
to predictions.
Various methods process the numbers of true/false positives and negatives, defining the
different ground truth-based metrics used by the selected papers. Accuracy is the most popular
of these metrics, as shown in Fig. 12. It measures the ratio of true predictions over all samples.
Confusion matrices often accompany accuracy-based evaluation to provide more insights into the
prediction behavior of certain classes.16 A disadvantage of accuracy is that it does not consider
the relative distribution of positive and negative samples. Other metrics such as recall (sensitivity
or true positive (TP) rate) and precision (purity) do consider data distribution. Recall and pre-
cision should be used when the cost of false negative (FN) and FP are high, respectively.
Nuisance rate and specificity (true negative (TN) rate) are related to precision because they
measure the effect of FPs (note that a lower nuisance rate is better) but in slightly different
ways. Altogether, 16 papers use metrics that penalize FPs more, whereas 13 papers use recall
Dehaerne et al.: Scanning electron microscopy-based automatic defect inspection. . .
section: ML. Supervised DL is most popular for classification which intuitively makes sense since accu- > 5.1 Ground Truth Comparison Metrics
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
that penalizes FNs. This suggests that more papers are interested in reducing FPs than FNs.
This is in line with the focus of avoiding nuisance defect predictions for many algorithms
(see Sec. 4.1.1). The F1 score is defined as the harmonic mean of recall and precision, acting
similarly to accuracy in balanced data distributions. The appropriate ground truth comparison-
based metric depends on the distribution of positive and negative samples in the dataset and the
relative cost of false predictions of both.
Average precision (AP) is a popular ground truth-based metric for DL-based instance locali-
zation and classification.54,88,98–100,102,104,106,111 Although all of the previously mentioned metrics
can also be used for localization, they often do not use well-defined criteria for when a locali-
zation prediction should be considered a TP/FP/TN/FN prediction. This is not the case for AP.
Despite its name, AP involves much more than averaging precision values. Given bounding-box
or instance segmentation predictions with confidence scores, the steps to calculate an AP score
are as follows:

content:
1. Calculate the intersection over union (IoU) between predictions and ground truth

instances. Figure 13 shows how IoU is calculated from bounding boxes. Segmentation
IoU is calculated similarly and used as a metric on its own for one of the selected
papers.17 Predictions are considered to be TP if they have an IoU score above a

are overlayed on the image as well as the box intersecting them. The IoU formula is shown where
AðXÞ is the area of bounding box X.

predetermined threshold and FP otherwise. Ground truth instances not associated with
a TP prediction are considered to be FN.

content:
2. Precision and recall are calculated using the TP and FN values from the previous step.

These precision and recall values with associated confidence scores are then used to create
a precision-recall curve. This removes the dependency of choosing a confidence threshold
for evaluation, although usually predictions with low confidence values are discarded even
before AP calculation.
section: 2. Precision and recall are calculated using the TP and FN values from the previous step.
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
3. The AP score is obtained by calculating the area under the precision-recall curve.

For localization combined with classification, an AP score is calculated for every class and
can then be combined into a mean AP (mAP) over all classes. It should be noted that there exists
a minimum area under precision-recall that is dependent on the class skew.112 This means that for
class imbalanced datasets, which are relatively common in defect inspection use cases,16,53,80,97
the mAP can be misleading because the range of possible AP values for different classes is not
the same.
section: 3. The AP score is obtained by calculating the area under the precision-recall curve.

content:
These metrics evaluate an algorithm based on computing aggregated statistics about the values of
its predictions (e.g., reporting a histogram of the predicted defect counts and pixel areas50).
Computing these statistics does not require access to ground truth labels. These metrics can
be subcategorized as measurement-based, where the predictions are continuous values most often
indicating geometric features, or prediction count-based, where the relative number of discrete
predictions of different categories are presented. Although these prediction statistics are gener-
ally useful for measuring the characteristics of the semiconductor products being inspected, they
do not directly measure algorithm performance. Only after analyzing the statistics and comparing
them with expected values from expert knowledge, for example, can these statistics be used to
measure the algorithm’s performance. However, we note that this approach is only useful in
evaluating the algorithm’s performance in aggregate and cannot assess individual predictions.
Therefore, we urge researchers to also report ground truth-based metrics by, for example, taking a
small subset of the total number of samples being predicted, assigning ground truths to these
samples, and using these to compare with the algorithm’s predictions. This illustrates that
statistics-based metrics are useful only with sufficient context.
section: 3. The AP score is obtained by calculating the area under the precision-recall curve. > 5.2 Prediction Statistics Metrics
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
5.3 Papers That Do Not Report Quantitative Metrics

Figure 14 shows that 40 studies did not report any quantitative evaluation of their discussed
algorithms. We believe that this is mainly due to the high levels of competition and confiden-
tiality in the semiconductor industry. We hope that thorough quantitative evaluation of inspection
algorithms will become more common in the literature.
6 Discussion of Research Questions
This section fully formulates and answers the four research questions (RQs) from Sec. 1.
We refer to categorizations and summarize findings from Secs. 3, 4, and 5. Other findings and
new ideas are introduced to fill knowledge gaps when appropriate.
section: 3. The AP score is obtained by calculating the area under the precision-recall curve. > 5.3 Papers That Do Not Report Quantitative Metrics

content:
6.1 What are the Key Components for SEM-Based Semiconductor Defect

Inspection Algorithms?
With this question, we aimed to identify the common building blocks of all inspection algorithms
and how the implementation of each component varies across different algorithm types. We
propose that every automated defect inspection algorithm consists of the following sequential
phases: (i) setup, (ii) pre-processing, (iii) feature extraction, and (iv) final prediction.
section: 3. The AP score is obtained by calculating the area under the precision-recall curve. > 6.1 What are the Key Components for SEM-Based Semiconductor Defect
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
6.1.1 Setup

We consider any processing that is done on data other than query images at prediction time as
part of the setup phase. A prototypical example is training an ML or DL model on training data.
Dehaerne et al.: Scanning electron microscopy-based automatic defect inspection. . .
section: 3. The AP score is obtained by calculating the area under the precision-recall curve. > 6.1 What are the Key Components for SEM-Based Semiconductor Defect > 6.1.1 Setup
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
As discussed in Secs. 4.2 and 4.3, there are various ways of training different models that can be
categorized as supervised or unsupervised. Once an appropriate model and training strategy has
been selected, most of the remaining work involves curating a training dataset. Two key prin-
ciples for training data often conflict with available resources. First, the more similar a training
dataset is to the target data at prediction time, the better the model’s performance will be. This
means that models should be retrained on new, more relevant datasets as they become available to
achieve maximum performance. However, each retraining may require significant manual dataset
curation and labeling along with the computation needed to retrain or fine-tune the model.
Second, the larger and more diverse the training dataset, the less the model will overfit specific
examples, improving predictions for unseen data samples. Advanced DL models with automatic
feature extraction capabilities require particularly large datasets to learn useful and generalizable
features. Transfer learning is popular for overcoming this problem. Keeping a feature extraction
network frozen, meaning that only the final prediction parts of the model are updated during
training, can further improve training convergence time.40 Overall, the setup phase is particularly
important for ML and DL models that must be trained on curated datasets.
NL methods also have setup phases, such as selecting appropriate algorithm parameter
values, handcrafting features, or selecting references. Threshold values are particularly sensitive
to changing imaging or processing conditions and should be reassessed or redefined in the setup
phase. An example of an important threshold value is minimum defect dimensions that will filter
out nuisance defect predictions caused by small feature variations. Similarly, edge detection algo-
rithms often rely on carefully selected threshold values for good results.52 In some cases, such as
classifying new and different defect types, modifying parameter values is not enough, and new
feature extraction operations must be added, which can be challenging and time-consuming.
Many reference-based NL methods also need to set up references before prediction time.
This can include design pattern region of interest identification,57 reference averaging,65 or
manually selecting an optimal reference image from a set of candidates. The exact manner and
extent of the setup changes to an NL algorithm will be determined from pattern design rules or
domain knowledge, often necessitating trial and error on a representative dataset to finetune
appropriately.
All inspection algorithms should also be evaluated before deployment. The selection of
the appropriate metric and how the metric is calculated, especially the collection of ground truth
data (as discussed in Sec. 5.1), should be considered an important part of the setup phase. For
supervised ML and DL algorithms, this follows from the labeled data they use and the learning
Fig. 14 Sunburst chart showing the number of papers that used different metric types. The sub-
types for ground truth comparison-based metrics are shown in Fig. 12.
Dehaerne et al.: Scanning electron microscopy-based automatic defect inspection. . .

J. Micro/Nanopattern. Mater. Metrol.

objectives they optimize, but for many unsupervised and NL methods, evaluation constitutes
a separate setup step.

content:
6.1.2 Pre-processing

Raw input images can be transformed into a similar image to improve subsequent feature extrac-
tion. Although this can include operations such as input data normalization (resizing, data type,
etc.), the most consequential types of pre-processing are image enhancement algorithms.
Image enhancement includes denoising,18,27,44,54,59,63,110 contrast enhancement,44,63 and distortion
correction.71 Another type of preprocessing used by a selected paper includes combining images
taken from MPSI into a single image with higher contrast.46 These pre-processing operations are
usually optional but can be helpful because they preserve the fundamental characteristics of
the image while highlighting signals of interest.
section: 3. The AP score is obtained by calculating the area under the precision-recall curve. > J. Micro/Nanopattern. Mater. Metrol. > 6.1.2 Pre-processing
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
Extracting machine-interpretable descriptions of images or regions is a critical component of
every automatic defect inspection system. NL and ML (excluding DL) methods use handcrafted
features. Difference images obtained through alignment, pixel-wise comparison, and thresholding
between query and reference images are examples of handcrafted image feature that facilitates
defect segmentation. Edges detected using an algorithm such as Canny113 may also facilitate
defect segmentation. Common classification task features include descriptions of segmented
areas such as area, perimeter, or average grayscale value.42,84
Advanced DNNs automatically learn to extract hierarchical, complex, and abstract features
from images during training, often in an end-to-end manner with a feature classification network.
Although these learned features are useful for subsequent feature classifiers, they are largely
uninterpretable for humans. This is unlike NL methods where the feature extraction operations
are created, and thus interpretable, by human experts.37 To make the features extracted by their
DNN more interpretable, one paper93 used a special DNN architecture, namely class activation
maps,107 which gives insight into which regions of an image contributed most to a classification
output. Another special case is autoencoder DNNs for reference image generation.31,108 The gen-
erated reference images are interpretable and are usually followed by additional NL feature
extraction which is also interpretable. Still, a key limitation of the automatic feature extraction
capabilities of most DL-based inspection methods is its lack of human interpretability of all
the features in the many layers of DNNs.
section: 3. The AP score is obtained by calculating the area under the precision-recall curve. > J. Micro/Nanopattern. Mater. Metrol. > 6.1.3 Feature extraction
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
Predicting a task-specific output from the extracted features is the final component of an inspec-
tion algorithm. This is rather straightforward for supervised ML models, which take the features
as input to the trained model and produce an output corresponding to the expected label.
Similarly, supervised DL models use a (part of a) DNN to extract image features and then feed
them to another (part of the) DNN to produce a label prediction. Unsupervised ML and DL
algorithms are different in the sense that the prediction is not associated with a label but rather
to (dis-)similarity with the training data31,35,49,79,82–84,87,108 or heuristics.96,109,110 For many NL
segmentation methods, this is where segmentation maps are refined to exclude nuisance defect
predictions17,68,72,114 or connect separated defect segmentation components.19,25 Final prediction
is often a relatively simple but critical component of a defect inspection algorithm that must be
tuned most for particular inspection jobs.

content:
6.2 How Does the Context of the Manufacturing Process Affect Defect

Inspection Algorithms?
We propose that the maturity of the manufacturing process, or how far the process has progressed
on the yield learning curve,115 affects the availability of input data. This is a critical factor for
deciding on an appropriate algorithm type, as discussed at length in Sec. 4. Early in the yield
learning curve new devices and process flows are researched and developed. At this stage,
Dehaerne et al.: Scanning electron microscopy-based automatic defect inspection. . .
section: 3. The AP score is obtained by calculating the area under the precision-recall curve. > 6.2 How Does the Context of the Manufacturing Process Affect Defect
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
not much data have been collected and process parameters are changing relatively frequently,
which can render previously collected data unrepresentative for subsequent inspections. ML and
DL algorithms are most affected by this because it limits their training datasets and requires
frequent retraining to achieve good performance. This is especially true for supervised classi-
fication methods when a process change causes a new type of defect, which the model cannot
predict properly without retraining on data with these new defects. Similarly, for reference-based
algorithms, a defect-free reference will be harder to find and will need to be updated as the
process evolves. Generally, all inspection methods become more viable and performant as the
process matures towards high-volume manufacturing, with more available data and less variation
between subsequent inspection jobs on the same process.
We also propose that the output requirements of defect inspection algorithms are signifi-
cantly influenced by the maturity of the process. In the early stages of the yield learning curve,
there is a higher number and greater severity of defects. Consequently, inspection efforts need to
focus on detecting and classifying hard defects, which are anticipated to have a substantial
impact on yield.111 In later stages, the focus shifts to soft defects, which may or may not affect
device performance,111 as hard defects become increasingly rare and process parameters are fine-
tuned rather than undergoing drastic changes that could cause new types of defects. For NL
methods, adjusting detection and classification threshold values is crucial for targeting specific
types of defects. Tuning of ML and DL algorithms usually involves curating the training dataset,
especially its labels, to reflect the desired outputs, followed by retraining the models. In sum-
mary, as the yield ramps up, the overall defectivity decreases and the need for corresponding
process changes diminishes, impacting both the setup phase of the algorithms and their expected
performance.
section: 3. The AP score is obtained by calculating the area under the precision-recall curve. > J. Micro/Nanopattern. Mater. Metrol.
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
We propose that minimizing time-to-solution, the total time needed to complete a defect
inspection job from imaging to output prediction analysis, while delivering minimally viable
performance, is the key challenge of a defect inspection system. The inspection system can
theoretically be optimized to perfectly fit a specific use case but will not work on a similar but
different use case, prompting a long algorithm optimization process all over again. On the other
hand, a poor-performing inspection system will require extra work, often in the form of manual
image inspection, to correct false predictions, which adds substantially to the overall processing
time. Note that the performance level that could be considered minimally viable is often high
but varies depending on the exact scenario. The performance of defect inspection algorithms is
therefore of utmost importance but has already been discussed in this review. In the rest of
this subsection, we focus on the challenges relating specifically to the time aspect of the
time-to-solution problem.
section: 3. The AP score is obtained by calculating the area under the precision-recall curve. > 6.3 What are the Key Challenges for These Algorithms?
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
Often, the bottleneck for metrology of advanced nodes is SEM imaging due to its relatively low
throughput and high demand for many use cases. A tradeoff exists between the imaging through-
put and the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of the images obtained. Acquiring more frames of a
sample reduces noise in the average but requires more electrons to be fired at the sample, which
takes more time. High SNR generally gives better performance for defect inspection algorithms
so, ultimately, imaging recipes should be tuned to balance between imaging throughput and
inspection algorithm performance.33 Using algorithms that are more robust to low SNR allows
for higher throughput imaging. Two papers29,54 mention that DL methods are more robust to
noise than NL methods, whereas Neumann et al.31 explicitly included data of various noise levels
in their training set to make their CNN more robust to noise. Okuda and Hiroi66 and Oh et al.65
used averaging of aligned images to capture a golden reference image for defect comparison
while being able to image every individual image at high throughput rates. Image enhancement
algorithms (see Sec. 6.1.2) are another way to maintain defect detection performance while
relaxing image quality requirements, allowing for higher throughput imaging. Any algorithmic
techniques that make the tradeoff between inspection performance and imaging throughput more
favorable can significantly improve total time-to-solution.
Dehaerne et al.: Scanning electron microscopy-based automatic defect inspection. . .

source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
The setup phase in the algorithm deployment cycle (see Sec. 6.1.1) often requires manual input.
This manual work is especially expensive due to talent limitations in the SM industry.9 Ways to
(partially) automate the setup of ML and DL models include automatic feature selection (mainly
for non-DL models, see Sec. 4.2) and semi-supervised learning (see Sec. 4.3.1). Although not
discussed in any of the selected papers, we believe that having a labeled evaluation dataset for
NL methods could allow for simple brute-force or learning algorithms to predict initial parameter
values, potentially speeding up the manual tuning process. More generally, labeled evaluation
datasets can often be (partially) reused throughout the manufacturing optimization process,
enabling more repeatable and faster evaluations in subsequent inspections. This contrasts with
manual qualitative evaluations, which might be quicker in the short term but slower in the long
term due to having to perform many iterations.
section: 3. The AP score is obtained by calculating the area under the precision-recall curve. > J. Micro/Nanopattern. Mater. Metrol. > 6.3.2 Manual input during setup
source_file: Dehaerne et al. - 2025 - Scanning electron microscopy-based .json

content:
Processing throughput is the time needed to predict defectivity for all given images. An obvious
way to improve this is to use or design algorithms that can analyze images quickly, as many
selected papers claim.19,23,47,68,72,74,76,88,99,100,105,106,111 These design choices are quite specific to
each algorithm. Ultimately, processing throughput is determined by the type and amount of
available computational resources.
Graphics processing units (GPUs) specialize in matrix multiplication operations and paral-
lelization, greatly speeding up computation in many cases. DNNs are especially well-suited to
take advantage of these capabilities. At least an order of magnitude improvement in throughput
on GPU compared with the central processing unit (CPU) was reported in two selected papers.7,97
One NL method74 was implemented for improved throughput on GPUs and another68 proposed
a GPU implementation as future work to improve throughput. Although not mentioned in the
selected papers, many types of ML models have implementations that are designed for GPUs,
which can be especially beneficial for speeding up training.116–118
Many CPUs and GPUs can be used together in parallel along with significant memory and
storage resources in an HPC platform for maximum throughput and utilization. An example
of particularly memory-intensive algorithms that generally need to be run on HPCs are
design reference-based inspection algorithms, which are also referred to as die-to-database
algorithms.18,45,47,61,62 These algorithms process large files of design data, including pattern struc-
ture geometries and associated design rules, and batches of SEM images, often including their
imaging metadata, for inspection. In addition, many die-to-database algorithms also make use
of computationally intensive lithography simulators for synthesizing SEM references 55,56 or
estimating printed patterns on wafers from extracted contours on photomasks.46,56,119,120
These simulators should also be run on HPCs to keep execution times reasonable. Invest-
ment into acquiring and managing compute and memory resources is a prerequisite for running
advanced defect inspection algorithms at high throughput.

content:
6.4 What are Promising Future Research Directions for the Field?

To address significant gaps in the literature, we suggest the following three research directions for
the defect inspection algorithm research community.
